planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; mountain laurel blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after flowering and never remove more than one-third of the plant.

directions: Plant in well-drained, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial to light shade; mountain laurel is the state flower of Connecticut and Pennsylvania and one of the finest native flowering shrubs of the eastern woodlands, producing intricate, geometrically precise cup-and-saucer flowers in white, pink, bicolored, and banded forms in late spring. The flowers have a remarkable mechanism — the stamens are bent under tension and spring upright when touched by a bee, catapulting pollen onto the pollinator. All parts of the plant are toxic. It performs best in cool, moist, acidic woodland gardens and benefits from afternoon shade in zone 7–8. Named cultivars like 'Olympic Fire' (deep red buds, pink flowers), 'Elf' (compact dwarf), and 'Galaxy' (burgundy-red buds) offer exceptional garden forms. Mulch heavily with pine bark or oak leaves to maintain soil acidity and moisture.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; keep the crown at or above soil level and mulch to maintain moisture and acidity.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Florida azalea is one of the earliest and most fragrant of the native deciduous azaleas, producing clusters of brilliant golden-yellow to orange-yellow tubular flowers with exserted stamens in early spring before or with the emerging leaves. The fragrance is intensely sweet and carries far in the still spring air. It is native to the wooded ravines and stream banks of the Florida Panhandle, south Georgia, and Alabama and is one of the best native azaleas for Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens, tolerating the heat and humidity better than northern species. Plant as a foundation shrub, in a native garden, or massed along a woodland edge. Prune only immediately after bloom if needed; never shear. The color is exceptionally vivid when purchased in bloom.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade; fetterbush requires very acidic, waterlogged conditions and will not thrive in neutral or alkaline soil.

directions: Plant in strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.0), moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; fetterbush is a native evergreen shrub of pine flatwoods, pocosins, and bog margins across the coastal South, producing glossy, leathery, sharply ribbed leaves (the midrib runs parallel to the leaf margin — a key identification feature) and small pink to white urn-shaped flowers in early spring that are critically important for native mining bees and early bumblebee queens. The plant naturally grows in waterlogged, very acidic soil and is adapted to conditions that are difficult for most other plants. An outstanding native plant for bog gardens, wet acid borders, and naturally moist sandy soils in the Southeast. All parts are toxic to livestock and humans if ingested.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; do not confuse with moss phlox (Phlox subulata), which requires full sun — this species is the shade-tolerant woodland species.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; creeping phlox is the shade-tolerant native phlox species of the Southern Appalachian woodland floor, quite different from the sun-loving moss phlox (Phlox subulata) that cascades over walls in full sun. It produces fragrant lavender, pink, or blue-violet flowers in early spring just as trilliums and spring ephemerals are blooming, spreading by stolons to form a low, attractive mat of semi-evergreen foliage that brightens shaded areas year-round. It performs best in the cool, moist, shaded conditions of a woodland garden and is not suited to zone 9 and warmer. Named cultivars like 'Blue Ridge' (blue-violet) and 'Sherwood Purple' (vivid purple) are widely available from native plant nurseries.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in late winter or spring in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, rocky or caliche soil; this is a separate-sex species — both male and female trees are needed for fruit, though female trees can produce some fruit without pollination.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, rocky or caliche soil in full sun; Texas persimmon is a small native multi-trunked tree of the Texas Hill Country and Rio Grande Plains with distinctive smooth, peeling gray bark revealing white-green underbark (one of the most beautiful native bark patterns in the Southwest). The small round fruits ripen jet black in August–September and are intensely sweet when fully ripe, though the black pulp stains everything permanently — including lips, fingers, and clothing. They are eaten fresh, dried, or cooked into syrup and are an important food for coyotes, bears, gray foxes, and birds. The tree is extremely drought tolerant once established and requires alkaline, well-drained soil. Both male and female trees are needed for fruit production.
